ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for lobster pho. FOODON_00002700 (noodle soup food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP (noodle-based soup) is the closest parent. No specific lobster pho code exists in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for lobster pho or Vietnamese noodle soup at this specificity level.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard bowl of lobster pho weighing approximately 550 g, comprising broth, rice noodles, lobster pieces, and fresh herb garnish.

nutrient_source.title: Pho (Vietnamese noodle soup with beef) — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with lobster nutrient data from USDA FoodData Central (Northern lobster, cooked, moist heat, NDB 15147) and HPB Singapore nutrient reference for pho-style noodle soups
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Health Promotion Board Singapore
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for lobster pho. Values are estimated by combining: (1) USDA SR Legacy pho/Vietnamese noodle soup base (broth + rice noodles + herbs, ~65% of dish weight), (2) USDA FoodData Central cooked lobster data (NDB 15147, ~20% of dish weight), and (3) HPB Singapore reference values for pho-style dishes. The lobster substitution increases protein and cholesterol relative to standard beef pho. Per-100g values represent the assembled dish as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — rice noodles and Vietnamese pho analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for lobster pho. GI estimated at 40 based on: (1) rice noodles (bánh phở) GI ~40–53 per University of Sydney GI Database (rice vermicelli, boiled, GI=58 adjusted downward for thicker flat noodles and broth dilution effect); (2) the dominant carbohydrate source is rice noodles at approximately 8.5g carbohydrate per 100g of assembled dish; (3) the protein and fat content from lobster and broth further attenuate glycemic response. GI of 40 is a conservative low estimate consistent with published pho GI analogues. GL per serving = (40 × 46.75g available carbohydrate per 550g serving) / 100 = 18.7, rounded to 18.8.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from fish sauce and slow-simmered bone broth
hypertension.guidance: A single 550g bowl of lobster pho provides approximately 2310 mg sodium, which represents over 100% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced fish sauce, consume only partial broth, and limit frequency to occasional consumption.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load from rice noodles, partially offset by protein and fat from lobster
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated glycemic load of approximately 18.8 per serving is moderate; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor portion size of rice noodles, pair with additional non-starchy vegetables, and note that the high protein content from lobster (~37g per serving) helps moderate postprandial glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated sodium and moderate cholesterol from lobster, partially offset by low saturated fat
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Lobster pho contains approximately 55 mg cholesterol per 100g and 420 mg sodium per 100g; while saturated fat is low at 0.4g per 100g, the high sodium load from the broth poses a significant cardiovascular risk, and individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit broth consumption and choose this dish only occasionally.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lobster Pho (Phở Tôm Hùm) is a premium Vietnamese noodle soup dish served in Singapore, featuring whole or halved fresh lobster as the centrepiece protein in place of the traditional beef or chicken. The dish is built on a deeply aromatic, slow-simmered broth prepared with charred ginger and onion, star anise, cinnamon, cloves, and fish sauce — the hallmark flavour base of Vietnamese pho. Flat rice noodles (bánh phở) are served in the broth and topped with the cooked lobster, bean sprouts, fresh Thai basil, coriander, mint, sliced chilli, and lime wedges. The result is a dish that balances the clean, sweet umami of lobster with the complex spiced warmth of pho broth. Nutritionally, lobster pho is a high-protein, moderate-carbohydrate dish with low saturated fat. Per 100g, it provides approximately 72 kcal, 6.8g protein, 1.4g fat, and 8.5g carbohydrate. The primary nutritional concern is sodium, with approximately 420 mg per 100g derived from fish sauce and bone broth, making a full bowl a high-sodium meal. The glycemic index is estimated at 40 (low), as the rice noodles are the main carbohydrate source but are moderated by the protein and fat content of the dish. Lobster is a rich source of lean protein, selenium, zinc, vitamin B12, and phosphorus. This dish is popular in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore and is considered a luxury item due to the premium cost of fresh lobster. It is suitable for halal diets and is dairy-free, but contains crustacean and fish allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in lobster pho?
Is lobster pho high in sodium?
What are the ingredients in lobster pho?
How much protein is in lobster pho?
What is the GI of pho?

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for lobster pho as a composite dish. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA FoodData Central data for cooked lobster (NDB 15147) with USDA SR Legacy and HPB Singapore reference values for Vietnamese pho-style noodle soup. The estimation methodology is transparent and defensible but does not constitute an exact match to a primary source entry for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data: USDA FoodData Central cooked lobster (NDB 15147) combined with USDA SR Legacy pho/Vietnamese noodle soup base and HPB Singapore pho reference. No single approved-source entry exists for lobster pho. 2. Sodium content is high due to fish sauce and bone broth; actual sodium may vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 3. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate based on rice noodle GI data from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for lobster pho exists in the literature. 4. GL per serving calculated as: GI (40) × available carbohydrate per serving (46.75g for 550g serving at 8.5g carb/100g) / 100 = 18.7, reported as 18.8. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central lobster entry or HPB Singapore pho reference. 6. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the primary source data used for this estimation. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in lobster and rice noodle components per USDA FoodData Central. 8. This dish is served at Viet'Spice - Hương vị Việt - Circular Road on field survey data at SGD 31.90. 9. Cuisine classified as Vietnamese-Singaporean reflecting the Vietnamese culinary tradition served in a Singapore restaurant context. 10. Halal status is assumed based on typical preparation without pork or alcohol; individual restaurant verification recommended.
